name: twitter-publisher
notes:
SETUP GUIDE

1. HOW TO IMPORT
   n8n Settings → Workflows → Import → paste this JSON

2. CREDENTIALS TO CONFIGURE
   - Twitter OAuth 1.0a: n8n Credentials → New → Twitter OAuth1 API
     Name it exactly: 'Twitter Content Publisher'
     Required fields:
       Consumer Key (API Key): from developer.twitter.com → your app → Keys and Tokens
       Consumer Secret (API Key Secret): from same location
       Access Token: from developer.twitter.com → Keys and Tokens → Access Token (your posting account)
       Access Token Secret: from same location

3. TWITTER DEVELOPER APP SETUP (one-time)
   a. Go to developer.twitter.com → Projects & Apps → Add App → Free tier
   b. App settings → User authentication settings → App permissions → Read and write
      (Write permission is REQUIRED for POST /2/tweets — read-only apps cannot post)
   c. Keys and Tokens → Generate Access Token and Secret
      These tokens are scoped to YOUR account (the account that will post the threads)
   d. Note the API Key and API Key Secret (Consumer Key/Secret)

4. RATE LIMIT AWARENESS (CRITICAL)
   - Free tier hard limit: 500 posts/month
   - A 5-tweet thread = 5 posts against the monthly limit
   - A 10-tweet thread = 10 posts against the monthly limit
   - At 1 video/week with 5-tweet threads: 20 posts/month from threads
   - Remaining: ~480 posts/month for standalone tweets, replies, other content
   - Monitor usage at: developer.twitter.com → your app → Rate Limits dashboard
   - IMPORTANT: There is no automated usage tracking in this workflow — monitor manually

5. THREAD CHAINING PATTERN
   First tweet posts standalone. Each subsequent tweet replies to the previous one
   using the in_reply_to_tweet_id field. This creates a proper Twitter thread where
   all replies appear nested under the first tweet in readers' timelines.

6. HOW TO ACTIVATE
   After credential setup: Toggle the workflow to Active (top-right switch in n8n)
   This workflow triggers automatically when content_bank.status changes to 'approved'

nodes.name: Supabase Trigger (approved status)
nodes.notes: Watches content_bank table for INSERT and UPDATE events. The IF filter below restricts to status=approved rows only. Same trigger pattern as youtube-publisher — both run independently when content is approved.

nodes.name: Filter: status = approved
nodes.notes: Only proceed when content_bank row status = 'approved'. This prevents accidental early publishing if status changes to other values. Twitter thread only fires on the approved transition.

nodes.name: Filter: has twitter array
nodes.notes: Only proceed if platform_angles.twitter is a non-empty array. If AI content generation hasn't run yet or produced no tweets, skip gracefully rather than failing.

nodes.name: Code: Validate and Format Tweet Array
nodes.notes: Validates the tweet array: trims whitespace, truncates tweets over 280 chars to 277 + '...', filters empty strings. Logs rate limit context. Splits into first_tweet (standalone) and remaining_tweets (will be chained as replies). The 500 posts/month limit is a Free tier hard cap — no automated tracking in v1.

nodes.name: HTTP Request: Post First Tweet
nodes.notes: Posts the first tweet as a standalone tweet (no reply_to). This is the thread opener. The response data.id is the tweet ID that subsequent tweets will reply to via in_reply_to_tweet_id. Rate limit: counts as 1 post against the 500/month Free tier allowance.

nodes.name: Code: Store First Tweet ID
nodes.notes: Extracts the first tweet's ID from the Twitter API response and constructs the thread URL. The first_tweet_id is passed to the loop that chains subsequent tweets as replies using in_reply_to_tweet_id.

nodes.name: Filter: has remaining tweets
nodes.notes: If the thread only has 1 tweet, skip the loop entirely and go directly to Supabase storage. Otherwise, proceed to chain the remaining tweets as replies.

nodes.name: SplitInBatches: Remaining Tweets (1 at a time)
nodes.notes: Processes remaining tweets one at a time. Each tweet must be posted sequentially with the previous tweet's ID as the in_reply_to_tweet_id to form a proper thread chain. Batch size of 1 ensures strict sequential ordering.

nodes.name: Code: Extract Current Tweet for Reply
nodes.notes: Extracts the current tweet text and sets the reply_to_tweet_id. In this implementation, all reply tweets chain back to the first tweet (flat thread structure). For a nested thread structure (each tweet replies to the previous), the previous tweet's ID would need to be tracked through loop iterations.

nodes.name: Wait: 1 second between tweets
nodes.notes: 1-second delay between tweet posts. Twitter API v2 Free tier does not explicitly document per-request rate limits for POST /2/tweets, but a 1-second delay provides safety margin against burst rejection (HTTP 429 Too Many Requests).

nodes.name: HTTP Request: Post Reply Tweet (in_reply_to_tweet_id)
nodes.notes: Posts each reply tweet using in_reply_to_tweet_id to chain it to the first tweet. This creates the thread structure visible in Twitter timelines. Each call counts as 1 post against the 500/month Free tier allowance. The body includes both text and reply.in_reply_to_tweet_id per Twitter API v2 spec.

nodes.name: Supabase: Store Thread URL

fieldValues.fieldValue: ={{ JSON.stringify({ ...$('Supabase Trigger (approved status)').item.json.visual_assets, twitter_thread_url: $json.thread_url, twitter_posted_at: new Date().toISOString(), twitter_tweet_count: $json.tweet_count }) }}

nodes.notes: Stores the Twitter thread URL and post metadata in content_bank.visual_assets. Logs: twitter_thread_url (link to the first tweet), twitter_posted_at (UTC timestamp), twitter_tweet_count (total posts used from 500/month allowance). This provides an audit trail for monthly rate limit tracking.

main.node: Code: Validate and Format Tweet Array
SplitInBatches: Remaining Tweets (1 at a time)
Code: Extract Current Tweet for Reply
SplitInBatches: Remaining Tweets (1 at a time)
